title: Adjusting Imports of Pharmaceuticals and Pharmaceutical Ingredients Into the United States
summary: The President has issued Proclamation 11020, adjusting imports of pharmaceuticals and pharmaceutical ingredients into the United States due to national security concerns. The Secretary of Commerce's investigation found that current import levels and circumstances threaten to impair U.S. national security, despite the U.S. being a leader in pharmaceutical R&D. The nation's heavy reliance on imports, with 53% of patented pharmaceuticals and 85% of patented Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) produced abroad, poses a risk to access to life-saving medications during global supply chain disruptions. The proclamation imposes a 100% ad valorem duty on imports of patented pharmaceuticals and associated ingredients, with exceptions. Companies committed to onshoring production will face a 20% duty, increasing to 100% in four years. Specific agreements with the European Union, Japan, South Korea, Switzerland, Liechtenstein, and the United Kingdom will also influence tariff rates. Imports from companies with approved onshoring plans and Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) pricing agreements will have a zero tariff until January 20, 2029. Generic pharmaceuticals and their ingredients are currently excluded from these tariffs. The proclamation also establishes procedures for monitoring and enforcing onshoring commitments, with potential for re-imposed tariffs and penalties for non-compliance. These measures are effective from July 31, 2026, and September 29, 2026, depending on the company.
